title: Likelihood Profiling for NLME Models
text: Wald-based confidence intervals (CIs), default output nlmixr2, rely assumption parameter estimates normally distributed. parameters near boundaries, variance components random effects, assumption often breaks resulting CIs can misleading. Likelihood profiling provides reliable alternative. Rather approximating likelihood surface quadratic, evaluates actual objective function (OFV) grid parameter values, holding parameter fixed turn re-estimating rest. parameter’s confidence interval set values whose OFV increase relative minimum threshold — default χ0.95,12≈3.84\chi^2_{0.95,1} \approx 3.84. nlmixr2extra provides two profiling methods via profile() generic:

title: Likelihood Profiling for NLME Models
text: use single-dose theophylline dataset (theo_sd) nlmixr2data.
code: library(nlmixr2extra) library(ggplot2)  oneCmt <- function() {   ini({     tka  <- log(1.57)   # log absorption rate constant     tcl  <- log(2.72)   # log clearance     tv   <- log(31.5)   # log volume (fixed)     eta.ka ~ 0.6     eta.cl ~ 0.09     add.sd <- 0.7   })   model({     ka <- exp(tka + eta.ka)     cl <- exp(tcl + eta.cl)     v  <- exp(tv)     cp <- linCmt()     cp ~ add(add.sd)   }) }  fit <- nlmixr2(oneCmt, data = nlmixr2data::theo_sd,                est = "focei", control = list(print = 0)) fit

title: Likelihood Profiling for NLME Models
Likelihood Profiling for NLME Models
Likelihood Profiling for NLME Models
text: profileLlp() uses adaptive algorithm: Start point estimate step directions. step, fix target parameter re-estimate others. Stop OFV change within ofvtol desired ofvIncrease, next parameter step change rounded value paramDigits significant figures.

title: Likelihood Profiling for NLME Models
text: LLP settings set via llpControl(): get 90% CI instead 95%, lower threshold:
code: # Default settings ctrl <- llpControl(   ofvIncrease   = qchisq(0.95, df = 1),  # ~3.84 for 95% CI   rseTheta      = 30,    # starting step size as % of the estimate   itermax       = 10,    # max iterations per direction   ofvtol        = 0.005, # tolerance on OFV difference   paramDigits   = 3      # significant digits for convergence )  profTka <- profile(fit, which = "tka", control = ctrl) profTka90 <- profile(fit, which = "tka",                        control = list(ofvIncrease = qchisq(0.90, df = 1)))

title: Likelihood Profiling for NLME Models
text: returned data frame one row per model evaluation. Key columns: Parameter — parameter fixed row OFV — objective function value step profileBound — present boundary rows; absolute value ofvIncrease threshold sign indicates lower (-) upper (+) One column per model parameter showing estimate step rows profileBound non-NA give profile CI limits directly tka column.
code: # Show rows near the boundary profTka[!is.na(profTka$profileBound), ]

title: Likelihood Profiling for NLME Models
text: profile data frame contains everything needed profile plot. standard presentation shows OFV – OFV_min y-axis parameter value x-axis, horizontal reference line CI threshold. symmetric, approximately parabolic profile indicates Wald CI reliable. asymmetric flat profile indicates non-normality; cases profile CI trustworthy.
code: # Compute ΔOFV relative to the minimum ofvMin <- min(profTka$OFV, na.rm = TRUE) profTka$dOFV <- profTka$OFV - ofvMin  # The two boundary rows (one per direction) bounds <- profTka[!is.na(profTka$profileBound), ]  ggplot(profTka, aes(x = tka, y = dOFV)) +   geom_line() +   geom_point() +   geom_hline(yintercept = qchisq(0.95, 1), linetype = "dashed", colour = "red") +   geom_vline(xintercept = bounds$tka, linetype = "dotted", colour = "blue") +   labs(     x     = "tka",     y     = expression(Delta * "OFV"),     title = "Likelihood profile for tka",     subtitle = sprintf(       "95%% profile CI: [%.3f, %.3f]",       min(bounds$tka), max(bounds$tka)     )   ) +   theme_bw()

title: Likelihood Profiling for NLME Models
text: profileFixed() evaluates OFV user-supplied grid rather searching adaptively. useful want reproduce specific grid (e.g. table plot comparing multiple models) adaptive algorithm struggles.

title: Likelihood Profiling for NLME Models
code: # Evaluate tka at seven equally spaced values around the point estimate tkaEst <- fit$theta["tka"]  grid <- data.frame(tka = seq(tkaEst - 0.6, tkaEst + 0.6, length.out = 7))  profGrid <- profile(fit, which = grid, method = "fixed") profGrid[, c("Parameter", "tka", "OFV")]

title: Likelihood Profiling for NLME Models
text: profileFixed() accepts data frame multiple columns. row fixes named parameters simultaneously, allowing trace joint profile contours.
code: grid2d <- expand.grid(   tka = seq(tkaEst - 0.4, tkaEst + 0.4, length.out = 5),   tcl = seq(nlmixr2est::fixef(fit)[["tcl"]] - 0.2,             nlmixr2est::fixef(fit)[["tcl"]] + 0.2, length.out = 5) )  profJoint <- profile(fit, which = grid2d, method = "fixed")  ggplot(profJoint, aes(x = tka, y = tcl,                        fill = OFV - min(OFV, na.rm = TRUE))) +   geom_tile() +   scale_fill_viridis_c(name = expression(Delta * "OFV")) +   geom_contour(aes(z = OFV - min(OFV, na.rm = TRUE)),                breaks = qchisq(0.95, 2), colour = "white") +   labs(title = "Joint profile: tka × tcl",        subtitle = "White contour = 95% joint confidence region") +   theme_bw()

what: Comparing profile CIs with Wald CIs
title: Likelihood Profiling for NLME Models
text: Profile CIs wider Wald CIs likelihood surface asymmetric. common variance parameters log-scale parameters near zero.
code: # Wald CI from the fit waldCi <- confint(fit)  # uses the covariance matrix  # Extract profile CI from the boundary rows boundRows <- profAll[!is.na(profAll$profileBound), ]  # For each parameter, the two boundRows give lower and upper limits profileCi <- tapply(   seq_len(nrow(boundRows)),   boundRows$Parameter,   function(idx) {     vals <- boundRows[idx, boundRows$Parameter[idx[1]]]     c(lower = min(vals), upper = max(vals))   } )  # Show side-by-side do.call(rbind, lapply(names(profileCi), function(p) {   data.frame(     parameter   = p,     wald_lower  = waldCi[p, 1],     wald_upper  = waldCi[p, 2],     prof_lower  = profileCi[[p]]["lower"],     prof_upper  = profileCi[[p]]["upper"]   ) }))

title: Likelihood Profiling for NLME Models
text: Start : profiling parameters time-consuming. Profile parameters primary interest first. Increase itermax see “aborted due many iterations”. default 10 conservative; 20–30 often sufficient variance parameters. Adjust rseTheta: initial step large (OFV jumps past target immediately) reduce rseTheta. small (many steps needed boundary reached) increase . Fixed-point fallback: profileLlp() fails converge particular parameter, profileFixed() coarse grid around estimate can give rough CI quickly.

title: Model Linearization for IIV and Residual Error Search
text: Fitting nonlinear mixed-effects (NLME) model every candidate random-effect residual-error structure computationally expensive. Model linearization replaces nonlinear model first-order Taylor expansion around current individual predictions. resulting linear model fits fraction time, allowing exhaustive search : Inter-individual variability (IIV) structure — parameters carry random effects, pairs correlated (iivSearch()). Residual error model — additive, proportional, combined variance structures (resSearch()). workflow three stages: Fit nonlinear model (method; least one η\eta). Linearize fit (linearize()). Search IIV residual structure linearized model, refit top candidates original nonlinear model (rerunTopN()).

title: Model Linearization for IIV and Residual Error Search
text: FOCEI objective function MAP estimates η̂\hat\eta_i : OFVi=−2log⁡p(yi|η̂)−2log⁡p(η̂) \text{OFV}_i = -2\log p(y_i|\hat\eta_i) - 2\log p(\hat\eta_i) first-order Taylor expansion f(t,η̂)f(t, \hat\eta_i) r(t,η̂)r(t, \hat\eta_i) (residual variance) around population prediction η̂=0\hat\eta_i = 0 gives: yi≈f0+∑k∂f∂ηk|η̂(ηk−η̂k)⏟BASE_TERMS+ε y_i \approx f_0 + \underbrace{\sum_k \frac{\partial f}{\partial\eta_k}\bigg|_{\hat\eta}\!\!(\eta_k - \hat\eta_k)}_{\text{BASE\_TERMS}} + \varepsilon ε∼𝒩(0,r02)\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\,r_0^2) linearized residual variance similarly expanded. “FOCE approximation”. Adding residual-variance gradient gives full “FOCEI approximation”. linearized model linear η\eta parameters, candidate structure requires one fast FOCEI fit rather full nonlinear optimization.

title: Model Linearization for IIV and Residual Error Search
code: library(nlmixr2extra) library(ggplot2)

title: Model Linearization for IIV and Residual Error Search
text: Define one-compartment model additive residual error IIV correlation CL V:
code: oneCmt <- function() {   ini({     tcl <- log(2.7) # Cl     tv <- log(30) # V     tka <- log(1.56) #  Ka     eta.cl + eta.v ~ sd(cor(0.3, 0.99, 0.5))     eta.ka ~ 0     add.sd <- 0.7   })   model({     ka <- exp(tka + eta.ka)     cl <- exp(tcl + eta.cl)     v <- exp(tv + eta.v)     d / dt(depot) <- -ka * depot     d / dt(center) <- ka * depot - cl / v * center     cp <- center / v     cp ~ add(add.sd)   })   }  set.seed(42) ev <- rxode2::et(amt = 300, cmt = "depot") |>   rxode2::et(time = c(0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 6, 8, 12, 16, 24)) sim <- rxode2::rxSolve(oneCmt, ev, nSub = 50, addDosing = TRUE) sim$dv <- sim$sim sim$id <- sim$sim.id sim <- sim[, c("id", "time", "amt", "dv", "evid")]

title: Model Linearization for IIV and Residual Error Search
text: Define base model IIV additive residual error:
code: oneCmtBase <- function() {   ini({       tcl <- log(2.7) # Cl       tv <- log(30) # V       tka <- log(1.56) #  Ka       add.sd <- 0.7     })     model({       ka <- exp(tka)       cl <- exp(tcl)       v <- exp(tv)       d / dt(depot) <- -ka * depot       d / dt(center) <- ka * depot - cl / v * center       cp <- center / v       cp ~ add(add.sd)     })   }

title: Model Linearization for IIV and Residual Error Search
text: Fit data base model oneCmtBase typical NLME method (e.g. FOCEI):
code: fit <- nlmixr2(oneCmtBase, sim, est = "focei")

title: Model Linearization for IIV and Residual Error Search
text: linearize() takes fitted nlmixr2 object returns nlmixr2Linearize fit. function: addEtas = TRUE, adds small fixed etas thetas re-evaluates model maxOuterIterations = 0 extract derivatives. Builds linearized model using linModGen(). Fits linear model via FOCEI vector mceta values, stopping relative OFV deviation within relTol.
code: # addEtas = TRUE adds fixed etas on every theta before linearizing, # giving derivatives with respect to every parameter. fitLin <- linearize(fit, addEtas = TRUE)

title: Model Linearization for IIV and Residual Error Search
text: linearize(), confirm linear approximation reproduces original model well: visual check available via linearizePlot(): plot shows original vs. linearized individual objective values etas. Points lie identity line good approximation.
code: match <- isLinearizeMatch(fitLin)  # OFV agreement (relative tolerance 10%) match$ofv[[1]]   # TRUE/FALSE if OFV differ by less than tol match$ofv[[2]]   # all.equal() message if FALSE   # Omega matrix agreement match$omega[[1]]  # TRUE/FALSE if Omega matrices differ by less than tol  # Individual eta agreement match$eta[[1]] # TRUE/FALSE if all individual etas differ by less than tol   # Residual variance agreement match$err[[1]] # TRUE/FALSE if residual variances differ by less than tol linearizePlot(fitLin)

title: Model Linearization for IIV and Residual Error Search
text: focei argument controls whether residual-variance gradient included: FOCEI accurate models heteroscedastic residuals (proportional combined error). FOCE faster stable additive-models.
code: # Force FOCE linearization fitLinFoce <- linearize(fit, addEtas = TRUE, focei = FALSE)  # Force FOCEI fitLinFocei <- linearize(fit, addEtas = TRUE, focei = TRUE)

title: Model Linearization for IIV and Residual Error Search
text: mceta argument vector Monte Carlo eta sample sizes tried order. algorithm stops relative OFV deviation within relTol: mceta = -1 uses exact gradient (Monte Carlo sampling) fastest option. Larger values useful exact gradients cause numerical issues.

Refit top 3 structures with the original model top3 <- rerunTopN(iivRes, n = 3) bestStructure <- top3$summary$search[which.min(top3$summary$O.BIC)] cat("Best IIV structure:", bestStructure, "\n")  ## 6. title: Model Linearization for IIV and Residual Error Search
text: Linearization fails converge (relTol exceeded) Try larger mceta values: mceta = c(100, 500, 2000). Switch focei = FALSE (FOCE) numerically stable. Increase relTol slightly (e.g. 0.30) looser quality threshold. iivSearch produces many NA entries Non-convergence common small degenerate IIV structures. NA rows skipped ranking; indicate bug. half search fails, check whether starting eta estimates (linearized fit) reasonable. isLinearizeMatch fails omegas omega matrix comparison uses MAP estimates linearized fit. Small discrepancies variance components expected; tolerance 20% (tol = 0.20) often appropriate omega terms. rerunTopN slow n calls nlmixr2() full nonlinear fit. reduce runtime, use coarser foceiControl() (e.g. fewer inner iterations) inside rerunTopN() screening, clean final fit selected structure.

title: nlmixr2 Algebraic Solutions with Formula
text: common use formula interface thorough QT (TQT) concentration-QT (C-QT) analysis. clinical question whether 10 msec ΔQT\Delta QT exceeded therapeutic drug concentrations. standard model : categorical effect nominal post-dose time (captures circadian food-related drift) continuous linear effect drug concentration (parameter interest) per-subject random intercept (baseline ΔQT\Delta QT offset) formula interface one call: b single intercept parameter; param decomposes fixed effect per timeF level plus linear slope conc. random per- subject offset bRe added b predictor.

previous_headings: Quick start > Worked example: concentration-QT analysis
title: nlmixr2 Algebraic Solutions with Formula
text: start one-compartment oral PK profile (Bateman equation), scale typical CmaxC_{max} 1000 ng/mL, apply per-subject log-normal scaling individual CmaxC_{max} values log-normally distributed. Now simulate dQT true model. true slope 0.01 msec/(ng/mL), produces 10 msec ΔQT\Delta QT population CmaxC_{max}. categorical time effects encode typical circadian drift pattern. simulation’s bRe SD 1 (formula interface’s current default random-effect starting values).
code: withr::with_seed(42, {   nSubj    <- 40   nomTimes <- c(0, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12)  # 10 nominal times    # Typical 1-compartment oral PK profile (unit Bateman), then scaled   # so the population Cmax is 1000 ng/mL   bateman    <- function(t, ka = 2, ke = 0.1) {     (ka / (ka - ke)) * (exp(-ke * t) - exp(-ka * t))   }   cmaxFactor <- 1000 / max(bateman(seq(0, 12, 0.01)))    # Per-subject log-normal scaling of the concentration profile   subjScale  <- exp(rnorm(nSubj, mean = 0, sd = 0.4))    cqtDesign <-     expand.grid(id = 1:nSubj, timeH = nomTimes)   cqtDesign$conc  <- bateman(cqtDesign$timeH) * cmaxFactor *                      subjScale[cqtDesign$id]   cqtDesign$timeF <- factor(cqtDesign$timeH,                             levels = as.character(nomTimes))   cqtDesign$dQT   <- 1  # placeholder; nlmixrFormula(est="rxSolve") fills it in }) cqtSimPrep <-   nlmixrFormula(     dQT ~ b + bRe ~ (bRe|id),     data  = cqtDesign,     # 10 time-level intercepts (relative to time 0) + slope on conc     start = list(       b     = c(0,         # timeF == 0  (pre-dose baseline)                 3, 5, 6, 5, 2, 0, -1, -2, -3,  # post-dose time effects                 0.01),     # slope on conc (msec per ng/mL)       addSd = 3     ),     param = list(b ~ timeF + conc),     est   = "rxSolve"   ) #> ℹ parameter labels from comments are typically ignored in non-interactive mode #> ℹ Need to run with the source intact to parse comments  cqtData <-   cqtSimPrep |>   as.data.frame() |>   select(id, conc, timeF, dQT = sim)  ggplot(cqtData, aes(x = conc, y = dQT, colour = timeF)) +   geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +   labs(x = "Concentration (ng/mL)", y = expression(Delta * "QT (msec)"))

title: Stabilizing Covariance Estimates with preconditionFit()
text: fitting nonlinear mixed-effects model FOCEI, standard errors (SEs) population parameters derived variance-covariance matrix parameter estimates. matrix assembled R matrix (second-derivative / Hessian approximation) , optionally, S matrix (cross-product first derivatives). parameters different scales, two parameters nearly collinear, R matrix becomes ill-conditioned: small rounding errors gradient calculations amplified matrix inversion resulting covariance matrix unreliable. Symptoms problem include: covMethod reported "r" "s" rather preferred "r,s" NaN negative values diagonal covariance matrix Standard errors implausibly large small relative estimates %RSE values large

title: Stabilizing Covariance Estimates with preconditionFit()
text: Aoki, Nordgren, Hooker (2016) showed simple linear reparameterization fixed-effects parameters can dramatically improve condition number R matrix without changing model fit. idea find matrix P P R P^T close identity matrix, run FOCEI reparameterized model (converges numerically stable covariance matrix), transform result back original parameterization. nlmixr2extra implements approach preconditionFit(). Reference: Aoki Y, Nordgren R, Hooker AC. Preconditioning Nonlinear Mixed Effects Models Stabilization Variance-Covariance Matrix Computations. AAPS J. 2016;18(2):505–518. doi:[10.1208/s12248-016-9866-5](https://doi.org/10.1208/s12248-016-9866-5)

title: Stabilizing Covariance Estimates with preconditionFit()
text: covariance method reported "r,s" (simply want robust covariance estimate), apply preconditioning: preconditionFit() modifies original fit object -place: call, fit$cov, fit$parFixedDf, fit$covMethod reflect preconditioned covariance. function returns preconditioned covariance matrix invisibly.
code: library(nlmixr2extra)  oneCompartment <- function() {   ini({     tka <- 0.45  # log Ka     tcl <- 1     # log Cl     tv  <- 3.45  # log V     eta.ka ~ 0.6     eta.cl ~ 0.3     eta.v  ~ 0.1     add.sd <- 0.7   })   model({     ka <- exp(tka + eta.ka)     cl <- exp(tcl + eta.cl)     v  <- exp(tv  + eta.v)     d/dt(depot)  <- -ka * depot     d/dt(center) <-  ka * depot - cl/v * center     cp <- center / v     cp ~ add(add.sd)   }) }  fit <- nlmixr2(oneCompartment, data = nlmixr2data::theo_sd,                est = "focei", control = list(print = 0)) fit preconditionFit(fit)

title: Stabilizing Covariance Estimates with preconditionFit()
text: estType argument determines much model re-estimated reparameterized problem:
code: # Only recompute the covariance matrix (fastest) preconditionFit(fit, estType = "none")  # Fix theta, re-estimate ETAs, then compute covariance preconditionFit(fit, estType = "posthoc")  # Full re-estimation on the reparameterized model (default) preconditionFit(fit, estType = "full")

title: Stabilizing Covariance Estimates with preconditionFit()
text: inner loop preconditionFit() iterates preconditioning reparameterized problem achieves "r,s" covariance (reliable type). target reached within ntry attempts function stops error. default ntry = 10. preconditionFit() fails even tries, R matrix may poorly determined preconditioning help. case inspect model identifiability problems consider likelihood profiling (profile(fit)) instead Wald-based confidence intervals.
code: # Allow up to 20 attempts before giving up preconditionFit(fit, ntry = 20L)

title: Stabilizing Covariance Estimates with preconditionFit()
text: preconditionFit() stores preconditioned covariance alongside previously computed covariances fit$covList. can inspect available switch setCov(): setCov() fit object updated -place displayed parameter table (fit$parFixedDf) reflects selected covariance.
code: # See which covariance estimates are stored names(fit$covList) # e.g. c("r,s", "precondition")  # Switch back to the standard r,s covariance setCov(fit, "r,s")  # Switch to the preconditioned covariance setCov(fit, "precondition")

what: Worked example: comparing SEs before and after preconditioning
title: Stabilizing Covariance Estimates with preconditionFit()
text: point estimates (Estimate, Back-transformed) random-effects summaries (BSV(CV%), Shrink(SD)%) identical — covariance-derived quantities (SE, %RSE, confidence intervals) change.
code: library(nlmixr2extra)  fit <- nlmixr2(oneCompartment, data = nlmixr2data::theo_sd,                est = "focei", control = list(print = 0))  # Record the original parameter table dfBefore <- fit$parFixedDf cat("Covariance method before:", fit$covMethod, "\n")  # Apply preconditioning (only recompute covariance, do not re-estimate) preconditionFit(fit, estType = "none") dfAfter <- fit$parFixedDf cat("Covariance method after:", fit$covMethod, "\n")  # Compare standard errors cbind(   SE_before = dfBefore$SE,   SE_after  = dfAfter$SE,   row.names = rownames(dfBefore) )

code: @Manual{,   title = {{nlmixr}: Nonlinear Mixed Effects Models in Population Pharmacokinetics and Pharmacodynamics},   author = {Matthew Fidler and Yuan Xiong and Rik Schoemaker and Justin Wilkins and Mirjam Trame and Richard Hooijmaijers and Teun Post and Wenping Wang},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 5.1.0},   url = {https://CRAN.R-project.org/package=nlmixr}, } @Article{,   title = {Nonlinear Mixed-Effects Model Development and Simulation Using nlmixr and Related R Open-Source Packages},   author = {Matthew Fidler and Justin Wilkins and Richard Hooijmaijers and Teun Post and Rik Schoemaker and Mirjam Trame and Yuan Xiong and Wenping Wang},   journal = {CPT: Pharmacometrics \& Systems Pharmacology},   year = {2019},   volume = {8},   pages = {621--633},   number = {9},   month = {sep},   abstract = {nlmixr is a free and open-source R package for fitting nonlinear pharmacokinetic (PK), pharmacodynamic (PD), joint PK-PD, and quantitative systems pharmacology mixed-effects models. Currently, nlmixr is capable of fitting both traditional compartmental PK models as well as more complex models implemented using ordinary differential equations. We believe that, over time, it will become a capable, credible alternative to commercial software tools, such as NONMEM, Monolix, and Phoenix NLME.},   address = {Hoboken},   publisher = {John Wiley and Sons Inc.},   url = {https://doi.org/10.1002/psp4.12445}, } @Article{,   title = {Performance of the SAEM and FOCEI Algorithms in the Open-Source, Nonlinear Mixed Effect Modeling Tool nlmixr},   author = {Rik Schoemaker and Matthew Fidler and Christian Laveille and Justin Wilkins and Richard Hooijmaijers and Teun Post and Mirjam Trame and Yuan Xiong and Wenping Wang},   journal = {CPT: Pharmacometrics \& Systems Pharmacology},   year = {2019},   volume = {8},   pages = {923--930},   number = {12},   month = {dec},   abstract = {The free and open-source package nlmixr implements pharmacometric nonlinear mixed effects model parameter estimation in R. It provides a uniform language to define pharmacometric models using ordinary differential equations. Performances of the stochastic approximation expectation-maximization (SAEM) and first order-conditional estimation with interaction (FOCEI) algorithms in nlmixr were compared with those found in the industry standards, Monolix and NONMEM, using the following two scenarios: a simple model fit to 500 sparsely sampled data sets and a range of more complex compartmental models with linear and nonlinear clearance fit to data sets with rich sampling. Estimation results obtained from nlmixr for FOCEI and SAEM matched the corresponding output from NONMEM/FOCEI and Monolix/SAEM closely both in terms of parameter estimates and associated standard errors. These results indicate that nlmixr may provide a viable alternative to existing tools for pharmacometric parameter estimation.},   url = {https://doi.org/10.1002/psp4.12471}, }

code: library(nlmixr2est) #> Loading required package: nlmixr2data library(nlmixr2extra) # basic example code # The basic model consists of an ini block that has initial estimates one.compartment <- function() {   ini({     tka <- 0.45; label("Absorption rate, Ka")     tcl <- 1; label("Clearance, Cl")     tv <- 3.45; label("Central volumne, V")     eta.ka ~ 0.6     eta.cl ~ 0.3     eta.v ~ 0.1     add.sd <- 0.7; label("Additive residual error")   })   # and a model block with the error specification and model specification   model({     ka <- exp(tka + eta.ka)     cl <- exp(tcl + eta.cl)     v <- exp(tv + eta.v)     d/dt(depot) = -ka * depot     d/dt(center) = ka * depot - cl / v * center     cp = center / v     cp ~ add(add.sd)   }) }  # The fit is performed by the function nlmixr/nlmixr2 specifying the model, data # and estimate (in a real estimate, nBurn and nEm would be much higher.) fit <- nlmixr2(one.compartment, theo_sd,  est="saem", saemControl(print=0, nBurn = 10, nEm = 20)) #> ℹ parameter labels from comments are typically ignored in non-interactive mode #> ℹ Need to run with the source intact to parse comments #> → loading into symengine environment... #> → pruning branches (`if`/`else`) of saem model... #> ✔ done #> → finding duplicate expressions in saem model... #> [====|====|====|====|====|====|====|====|====|====] 0:00:00 #> → optimizing duplicate expressions in saem model... #> [====|====|====|====|====|====|====|====|====|====] 0:00:00 #> ✔ done #> using C compiler: 'gcc.exe (GCC) 13.2.0' #> ℹ calculate uninformed etas #> ℹ done #> rxode2 3.0.2 using 8 threads (see ?getRxThreads) #>   no cache: create with `rxCreateCache()` #>  #> Attaching package: 'rxode2' #> The following objects are masked from 'package:nlmixr2est': #>  #>     boxCox, yeoJohnson #> Calculating covariance matrix #> → loading into symengine environment... #> → pruning branches (`if`/`else`) of saem model... #> ✔ done #> → finding duplicate expressions in saem predOnly model 0... #> → finding duplicate expressions in saem predOnly model 1... #> → optimizing duplicate expressions in saem predOnly model 1... #> → finding duplicate expressions in saem predOnly model 2... #> ✔ done #> using C compiler: 'gcc.exe (GCC) 13.2.0' #> → Calculating residuals/tables #> ✔ done #> → compress origData in nlmixr2 object, save 5952 #> → compress phiM in nlmixr2 object, save 3712 #> → compress parHistData in nlmixr2 object, save 2456 #> → compress saem0 in nlmixr2 object, save 27920  # In a real bootstrap, nboot would be much higher. fit2 <- suppressMessages(bootstrapFit(fit, nboot = 5)) fit2

text: fit nlmixr2 fit object nboot integer giving number bootstrapped models fit; default value 200 nSampIndiv integer specifying number samples bootstrapped sample; default number unique subjects original dataset stratVar Variable original dataset stratify ; useful distinguish sparse full sampling features may wish keep distinct bootstrap stdErrType gives standard error type updated standard errors; current possibilities : "perc" gives standard errors percentiles (default), "sd" gives standard errors using normal approximation mean standard devaition, "se" uses normal approximation standard errors calculated nSampIndiv ci Confidence interval level calculate.  Default 0.95 95 percent confidence interval pvalues vector pvalues indicating probability subject get selected; default value NULL implying probability subject restart boolean try restart interrupted incomplete boostrap.  default FALSE plotHist boolean indicating histogram plot assess well bootstrap .  default turned (FALSE) fitName fit name used name boostrap files.  default fit provided though something else. returnType describes return type model default returns updated model boostrapped confidence intervals standard errors updated estimates. fitList returns list full fits bootstrap. modelList returns model list (abbreviates parameters messages) used bootstrap summary statistics.

title: Bootstrap nlmixr2 fit — bootstrapFit
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{  oneCmt <- function() {   ini({     tka <- 0.45; label("Ka")     tcl <- 1; label("Cl")     tv <- 3.45; label("V")     eta.ka ~ 0.6     eta.cl ~ 0.3     eta.v ~ 0.1     add.sd <- 0.7   })   model({     ka <- exp(tka + eta.ka)     cl <- exp(tcl + eta.cl)     v <- exp(tv + eta.v)     linCmt() ~ add(add.sd)   }) }  fit <- nlmixr2(oneCmt, nlmixr2data::theo_sd, est = "saem", control = list(print = 0))  withr::with_tempdir({ # Run example in temp dir  bootstrapFit(fit, nboot = 5, restart = TRUE) # overwrites any of the existing data or model files bootstrapFit(fit, nboot = 7) # resumes fitting using the stored data and model files  # Note this resumes because the total number of bootstrap samples is not 10  bootstrapFit(fit, nboot=10)  # Note the boostrap standard error and variance/covariance matrix is retained. # If you wish to switch back you can change the covariance matrix by  nlmixr2est::setCov(fit, "linFim")  # And change it back again  nlmixr2est::setCov(fit, "boot10")  # This change will affect any simulations with uncertainty in their parameters  # You may also do a chi-square diagnostic plot check for the bootstrap with bootplot(fit) }) } # }

code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ oneCmt <- function() {   ini({     tka <- 0.45; label("Ka")     tcl <- log(c(0, 2.7, 100)); label("Cl")     tv <- 3.45; label("V")     eta.ka ~ 0.6     eta.cl ~ 0.3     eta.v ~ 0.1     add.sd <- 0.7   })   model({     ka <- exp(tka + eta.ka)     cl <- exp(tcl + eta.cl)     v <- exp(tv + eta.v)     linCmt() ~ add(add.sd)   }) }  fit <- nlmixr2(oneCmt, nlmixr2data::theo_sd, est = "saem", control = list(print = 0)) rxode2::.rxWithWd(tempdir(), {# with temporary directory  auto1 <- covarSearchAuto(fit, varsVec = c("ka", "cl"),     covarsVec = c("WT"))  })  ## Note that this didn't include sex, add it to dataset and restart model   d <- nlmixr2data::theo_sd d$SEX <-0 d$SEX[d$ID<=6] <-1  fit <- nlmixr2(oneCmt, d, est = "saem", control = list(print = 0))  # This would restart if for some reason the search crashed:  rxode2::.rxWithWd(tempdir(), {# with temporary directory  auto2 <- covarSearchAuto(fit, varsVec = c("ka", "cl"), covarsVec = c("WT"),                 catvarsVec= c("SEX"), restart = TRUE)  auto3 <- covarSearchAuto(fit, varsVec = c("ka", "cl"), covarsVec = c("WT"),                 catvarsVec=  c("SEX"), restart = TRUE,                 searchType = "forward") }) } # }
